questions.text: I tend not to detect when a story is being told for the benefit of a specific listener.
I find that maximizing my own earning potential is a responsibility I take seriously.
I experience moments of tenderness with such intensity that they can bring me to tears.
I pick up on the slight tightening of someone's jaw when they suppress a reaction.
I find that I'm rarely the most emotionally activated person in any given room.
I find that the felt weight of others' suffering is the moral experience that most clearly tells me which of my many possible actions would be most worth taking.
I tend not to notice when someone shifts their gaze away to signal they are done with a topic.
A beautiful piece of music can bring me to tears almost instantly.
I start my day open to whatever feels most compelling rather than consulting a schedule.
I hold the view that protecting my sleep schedule is more important than being available to others late at night.
I hold the view that the greatest reward is knowing I made someone else's life better.
I prefer to dive into a project and let the structure emerge organically.
I notice when a person swallows or clears their throat before saying something that is difficult for them.
I routinely set aside a portion of every paycheck specifically for helping others.
I pick up on when a thank-you carries genuine gratitude versus social routine.
I am the one who typically opens a meeting by framing the key question.
I feel that the moral responsibility generated by knowledge of suffering is not something I can opt out of without cost to my integrity.
I feel that inclusive deliberation prevents misguided effort.
I have cried from the sheer intensity of a sunset, a piece of art, or an act of kindness.
I feel uncomfortable when someone else is making decisions that I believe should be mine.
I think that my analytical approach to human pain allows me to contribute in ways that emotionally driven approaches cannot.
I let meetings flow conversationally rather than following a prepared agenda.
I tend not to register when a group's dynamic shifts because of a new piece of information.
I believe that the moral concern I feel when encountering suffering is an accurate perception of something real and important.
I feel that my personal goals are just as important as anyone else's needs.
I approach conflict by focusing on outcomes rather than on the emotions involved.
I plan my week on Sunday evening so Monday morning starts with clarity.
I believe that attention to detail is what separates good work from great work.
I hold the view that maintaining a measured, rational perspective during emotionally intense moments is a strength that serves the greater good.
I naturally position myself at the center of decision-making networks.
I am the type who shapes my contribution around the team's existing vision.
I feel comfortable contributing to something that bears the group's stamp, not mine.
I notice that I can compartmentalize emotional events and return to whatever I was doing without difficulty.
I find that my emotional reactions match the scale of the event rather than exceeding it.
I consolidate similar errands and tasks to minimize wasted time.
I find that I often forget my own needs because I am so focused on meeting the needs of those around me.
